HexSchool is about to launch a new service, and that service is related to what I'm currently doing. So this post is about the upcoming new service!
HexSchool has been hinting about a freelance project service for students to try, and I'm the first guinea pig (lol).

I officially started freelancing on 2019/07/29. The first company I worked with is Fable. Fable is a special kind of company — they don't hire their own engineers in-house. The whole company is PMs, and dev teams come from everywhere ~
Fable specializes in outsourced projects, so the dev team is actually quite large. There's a backend team, a copywriting team, a UI team, etc. I'm on the frontend team (
). I most often interact and communicate with backend developers, since data fetching / sending crosses our boundary. Copywriting and UI? Less interaction.
Below I'll talk about the communication tools we used, frontend team collaboration, and issues encountered.

Fable doesn't have in-house engineers — all dev folks are remote. Communication tool? Slack. So I'd recommend being able to use Slack. In my experience, at least 2 tech companies have used Slack for collaboration, and it's genuinely convenient for discussing code. Here's my earlier
After joining the freelance team, Fable adds you to their private Slack channels — Fable's PMs, frontend team, backend team, etc. The outsourced project's client PM is also in there (
so don't shitpost in the channel
I'm not sure how other teams collaborate, so I'll only describe the frontend team. Right now my frontend team is just me and the frontend lead — this guy ↓

During the project, I mostly follow Roy's direction — what to build tomorrow, next week, what testing to help with, etc. If we're waiting on data from the client or backend is mid-build, I tell Roy I'll handle some non-urgent stuff first.

I also habitually tell Roy what I'm about to commit before pushing. This makes it easier for the team lead to know what I did today and what I'm about to push, and also helps the lead report progress to the client ↓
When we hit dev issues or need help testing, we discuss and figure out how to help each other ↓
Account verification broken, mutual frustration QQ
Project timeline? This project has a timeline tracked in Google Sheets, but I can't share screenshots — too much business sensitivity inside.
Version control? GitHub with Git Flow. To build something, branch off
After Roy reviews the code, the feature is merged back into
. We don't really touch the Pull Request flow.

For freelancing, the thing most people care about is whether client requirements are clear. I ran into the issue too. When I first joined the frontend team, the spec required Laravel + Vue. Then the client wanted SPA, so we changed to SPA mode. Then the backend's system conflicted with our frontend, so we extracted the frontend into its own thing.
Extracting has upsides — cleaner frontend / backend separation. If you've done the
, you'd be at home in this setup. But it didn't last (huh?!) — the client later said the system needs SEO, so we switched to Nuxt.js.

So within two weeks of joining the team, the system architecture changed three times: LaravelVue → Vue CLI → Nuxt.js. You're probably thinking "won't this blow the timeline? And won't dev costs spike?" Fable negotiates timelines with the client, so timeline isn't a problem. The arch change happened because the client was still finding their business model, so arch changes were reasonable.

For me — I'd never touched Nuxt.js, let alone SSR — so did dev costs spike for me? Momentarily, yes. You can't just use existing Vue mental models for Nuxt.js — if you do, you'll hit walls everywhere. Mentally, I had to treat it as a totally new thing. I spent some time learning Nuxt.js and cramming SSR.
The project is still in dev — I still run into weird Nuxt.js issues — but the experience I'm gaining is irreplaceable. That's the practical industry experience a lot of people want.
